sections.title: 1. What is the Accusative Case?
sections.description: In a German sentence, the Nominative case is used for the Subject (the person or thing doing the action). The Accusative case is used for the Direct Object (the person or thing receiving the action).

golden_rule.title: The Golden Rule of Accusative
golden_rule.description: Only masculine nouns change their articles in the Accusative. Feminine, Neutral, and Plural nouns stay exactly the same as they are in the Nominative.

sections.title: 2. Using Definite and Indefinite Articles
sections.description: When you are talking about a specific item, you use Definite Articles (the). When you talk about "any" item or "one" item, you use Indefinite Articles (a/an).

neutral_feminine_examples.title: Examples with Neutral and Feminine (No Change)

examples.english: I drink a bottle of wine

sections.title: 3. Negative Articles (Kein)
sections.description: When you want to say "no" or "none" (negating a noun), you use kein. This follows the exact same pattern as "ein."

sections.title: 4. Asking Questions: "Wen" vs. "Was"
sections.description: When you ask a question about the direct object, the question word depends on whether you are talking about a person or a thing.

question_words.usage: Use this for things or objects

sections.title: 5. Why and When to Use It?
sections.description: The Accusative is triggered primarily by Transitive Verbs. These are verbs that require an object to make sense.

content.logic_summary: If you are performing an action on something (buying it, eating it, seeing it, having it), that "something" must be in the Accusative case. If that thing is masculine, add an -en to the article. If it is feminine or neutral, keep it as you learned it in the basic vocabulary list.

sections.title: 6. Context Example: The "Pet" Logic
sections.description: Notice how the case shifts depending on the word's role in the sentence:

examples.reason: Nominative because the dog is now the subject of the sentence

content.key_insight: This shows that the case is not about the word itself, but about the role the word plays in that specific sentence.
